name: tomb robbers
shortDescription: In ancient Egyptian mythology, tomb robbers were a significant concern, representing a challenge to the afterlife and cosmic order (Ma'at). While not deities themselves, their actions were viewed as a direct affront to the gods and the deceased.
description: Tomb robbers, though not deities in their own right, held a prominent, albeit negative, role in ancient Egyptian belief. The elaborate tombs constructed for pharaohs and nobles were intended to house their bodies and provisions for the afterlife, ensuring their successful journey through the Duat and eventual rebirth. Tomb robbers threatened this entire process. Their actions were considered a grave offense against Ma'at, the principle of cosmic order, justice, and truth. By desecrating the tombs and stealing the provisions meant for the deceased, they disrupted the afterlife journey and jeopardized the individual's chance at eternal life. The fear of tomb robbery was pervasive, leading to increasingly complex tomb designs, hidden chambers, and magical spells intended to ward off intruders. The punishments for tomb robbery were severe, reflecting the gravity of the offense. Ironically, some tomb robbers were likely temple officials or craftsmen involved in the original construction, possessing intimate knowledge of the tomb's layout and secrets. The constant struggle against tomb robbery highlights the Egyptians' profound belief in the afterlife and the importance of preserving the body and funerary goods for eternity. The presence of tomb robbers in the cultural consciousness demonstrates the constant battle between order and chaos, a central theme in Egyptian mythology. The actions of these individuals directly challenged the carefully constructed system of beliefs and rituals designed to ensure immortality.

sources.title: The Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: Translated by Raymond O. Faulkner

sources.title: Religion and Magic in Ancient Egypt
sources.author: Rosalie David

sources.title: Temples, Tombs and Hieroglyphs: A Popular Guide to Ancient Egypt
